./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:182:{"id":"corpus-c64e7d89a29e","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"correctness: reject malformed prompt envelopes missing id","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:331\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new probe heuristic only treats JSON as a DDx prompt envelope when `kind` is paired with `id`, `inputs`, `response_schema`, or `callback`. A malformed envelope like {\"kind\":\"prompt\",\"prompt\":\"Read main.go\"} is therefore treated as raw prompt text instead of failing validation, so execution proceeds to upstream-service selection. This violates the structured-I/O contract in the PRD and leaves invalid envelope input non-deterministic. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{\"kind\":\"prompt\",\"prompt\":\"Read main.go\"}' -upstream-service nonexistent` returns `config: unknown upstream-service \"nonexistent\"` instead of `invalid prompt envelope`.\\nSuggested fix: Treat objects that contain `kind` plus `prompt` as envelope candidates too, so missing required `id` still reaches envelope validation; add regression coverage for missing-id envelopes across inline, stdin, and @file inputs.","acceptance":"A JSON payload intended as a DDx prompt envelope but missing the required id field fails with an invalid prompt envelope error for -p, stdin, and @file input paths, while non-envelope JSON objects still pass through as raw prompt text; regression tests cover both behaviors.","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"bead":{"id":"hx-47258277","title":"correctness: reject malformed prompt envelopes missing id","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: cmd/ddx-agent/main.go:331\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new probe heuristic only treats JSON as a DDx prompt envelope when `kind` is paired with `id`, `inputs`, `response_schema`, or `callback`. This violates the structured-I/O contract in the PRD and leaves invalid envelope input non-deterministic. Repro: `go run ./cmd/ddx-agent -p '{\"kind\":\"prompt\",\"prompt\":\"Read main.go\"}' -upstream-service nonexistent` returns `config: unknown upstream-service \"nonexistent\"` instead of `invalid prompt envelope`.\\nSuggested fix: Treat objects that contain `kind` plus `prompt` as envelope candidates too, so missing required `id` still reaches envelope validation; add regression coverage for missing-id envelopes across inline, stdin, and @file inputs.","acceptance":"A JSON payload intended as a DDx prompt envelope but missing the required id field fails with an invalid prompt envelope error for -p, stdin, and @file input paths, while non-envelope JSON objects still pass through as raw prompt text; regression tests cover both behaviors.","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"]}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:186:{"id":"corpus-cbc4ba9c4924","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed focused bead with small acceptance surface","title":"Prevent execute-loop from launching open epics by default","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nAlpha validation run from /Users/erik/Projects/helix using `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json` against a live HELIX queue. Evidence includes the generated execution bundle `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7`, worktree `.ddx/.execute-bead-wt-helix-fef22846-20260411T011535-bbda19d7`, and the current execute-loop implementation in `cli/internal/bead/store.go` and `cli/internal/agent/execute_bead_loop.go`.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\n\n`ddx agent execute-loop` selected and launched an open `epic` bead from the HELIX execution queue instead of a bounded executable bead.\n\n## Reproduction\n1. In `/Users/erik/Projects/helix`, run `ddx bead ready --json --execution`.\n2. Observe the first candidate is `helix-fef22846`, `issue_type=epic`, title `Audit deleted HELIX artifact types for restoration or retirement`.\n3. Run `ddx agent execute-loop --once --json`.\n4. Observe DDx creates `.ddx/.execute-bead-wt-helix-fef22846-20260411T011535-bbda19d7` and `.ddx/executions/20260411T011535-bbda19d7/manifest.json` with `bead_id=helix-fef22846`.\n\n## Observed gap\n`ReadyExecution()` currently treats any open, unblocked bead as execution-ready unless `execution-eligible=false`, `superseded-by`, or cooldown metadata says otherwise. `execute-loop` then takes the first candidate without a stronger structural execution screen. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:190:{"id":"corpus-d1868d0e5457","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Update DDx to ddx-agent v0.1.1","description":"Upstream `DocumentDrivenDX/agent` now has point release `v0.1.1`. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:196:{"id":"corpus-d6e0eda51fa9","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Project home page is orphaned: add it to the sidebar and make the DDx logo a link","description":"## Observed\n\nThe project home page (`/nodes/.../projects/\u003cid\u003e/`) renders a useful overview — queue summary (ready/blocked/in-progress) plus three action cards (drain queue, re-align specs, run checks). User feedback: \"I see it when I navigate to a new project, then it disappears.\"\n\n## Root cause\n\n`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/NavShell.svelte:26-36` — the sidebar's `pages` array contains: beads, documents, graph, workers, sessions, personas, plugins, commits, efficacy. **There is no entry for the project home/overview.** Once a user clicks any sidebar link, there is no nav-level path back to the home page — only clicking the project in the `ProjectPicker` again, or editing the URL.\n\nAdjacent: the `\u003cspan\u003eDDx\u003c/span\u003e` brand in the header (`NavShell.svelte:61`) is not a link — common affordance for \"go home\" is missing.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\n1. Prepend a \"Overview\" (or \"Home\") entry to `pages` in `NavShell.svelte`, routing to `/nodes/\u003cnodeId\u003e/projects/\u003cprojectId\u003e/` (empty path segment after the project id). Use the `LayoutDashboard` icon that's already imported, or pick a dedicated home icon — design call.\n2. Wrap the `DDx` brand in an `\u003ca\u003e` that routes to `/` (node selector) or to the current project's home if one is selected. Pick one and stay consistent.\n\nTrivial change; the biggest decision is whether the brand link goes to node-root or project-home. Recommend project-home when a project is selected (less jarring) and node-root otherwise.","acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer on any project sub-page, I can always return to the project overview with a single click — either from a sidebar entry or from the DDx brand in the header.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. Sidebar has an \"Overview\" (or equivalent) entry at the top of the `pages` list, routing to `/nodes/\u003cnodeId\u003e/projects/\u003cprojectId\u003e/`.\n2. The entry is the active/highlighted one when the current URL matches the project-home route exactly (no trailing segment after `projectId`).\n3. DDx brand in the header is an anchor. When a project is selected, it routes to that project's home; otherwise routes to `/`.\n4. Playwright: navigate into `/beads`, click the Overview sidebar entry, assert URL is project home and queue summary renders. Then navigate into `/sessions`, click the DDx brand, assert URL is project home.\n5. No regression to any existing sidebar entry.","labels":["feat-008","ui","navigation"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-2513f08c","title":"Project home page is orphaned: add it to the sidebar and make the DDx logo a link","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"## Observed\n\nThe project home page (`/nodes/.../projects/\u003cid\u003e/`) renders a useful overview — queue summary (ready/blocked/in-progress) plus three action cards (drain queue, re-align specs, run checks). User feedback: \"I see it when I navigate to a new project, then it disappears.\"\n\n## Root cause\n\n`cli/internal/server/frontend/src/lib/components/NavShell.svelte:26-36` — the sidebar's `pages` array contains: beads, documents, graph, workers, sessions, personas, plugins, commits, efficacy. **There is no entry for the project home/overview.** Once a user clicks any sidebar link, there is no nav-level path back to the home page — only clicking the project in the `ProjectPicker` again, or editing the URL.\n\nAdjacent: the `\u003cspan\u003eDDx\u003c/span\u003e` brand in the header (`NavShell.svelte:61`) is not a link — common affordance for \"go home\" is missing.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\n1. Prepend a \"Overview\" (or \"Home\") entry to `pages` in `NavShell.svelte`, routing to `/nodes/\u003cnodeId\u003e/projects/\u003cprojectId\u003e/` (empty path segment after the project id). Use the `LayoutDashboard` icon that's already imported, or pick a dedicated home icon — design call.\n2. Wrap the `DDx` brand in an `\u003ca\u003e` that routes to `/` (node selector) or to the current project's home if one is selected. Pick one and stay consistent.\n\nTrivial change; the biggest decision is whether the brand link goes to node-root or project-home. Recommend project-home when a project is selected (less jarring) and node-root otherwise.","acceptance":"**User story:** As a developer on any project sub-page, I can always return to the project overview with a single click — either from a sidebar entry or from the DDx brand in the header.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. The entry is the active/highlighted one when the current URL matches the project-home route exactly (no trailing segment after `projectId`).\n3. DDx brand in the header is an anchor. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:198:{"id":"corpus-d945d6d80993","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Move session-log persistence fully behind service execution","description":"cmd/agent/service_session_log_adapter.go currently decodes public service events and recreates internal session start/end records using internal/core and internal/session. That gives the CLI private knowledge of the session-log schema and duplicates lifecycle persistence logic that should be owned by the service. CONTRACT-003 now says SessionLogDir chooses storage location but session-log persistence is service-owned.\n\nImplement that ownership in code. Native and subprocess execution should write the authoritative lifecycle and terminal records from inside the service execution path, including cancellation/error/final attribution. The CLI should consume the public event stream and returned session-log path only; it must not synthesize session-start/session-end records or depend on internal session-log payload types. Preserve enough information for replay, usage aggregation, and execute-bead evidence bundles.\n\nIn-scope files:\n- cmd/agent/service_session_log_adapter.go\n- service_execute.go and any session-log helpers it depends on\n- session-log/replay/usage tests affected by the ownership move\n- execute-bead/native logging regression tests such as agent-b9bd700f where appropriate\n\nOut of scope:\n- Broader benchmark trace persistence improvements beyond what is needed to make service-owned logs authoritative\n- CLI route/upstream-service cleanup outside session-log ownership","acceptance":"1. Service-owned execution writes the authoritative session lifecycle records; cmd/agent no longer recreates session start/end records from decoded service events.\n2. Final results still expose session-log path and replay/usage flows continue to work against service-owned logs.\n3. Targeted tests pass: go test ./cmd/agent ./service* ./session/... -run \"Test(Session|Replay|Usage|Execute).*\"","labels":["area:service","area:logging","area:cli","kind:architecture"],"bead":{"id":"agent-7faa0edf","title":"Move session-log persistence fully behind service execution","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"cmd/agent/service_session_log_adapter.go currently decodes public service events and recreates internal session start/end records using internal/core and internal/session. That older code rejects 'type: lucebox' / 'type: vllm' from the user's live config.\n\nFailing tests captured (sample):\n\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:680 TestNewWaitsBrieflyForInvalidQuotaRefresh\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:721 TestNewStartupQuotaRefreshContinuesAfterTimeout\n  service_upstream-services_test.go:765 TestPrimaryQuotaRefreshWorkerRefreshesOnTimer\n  service_validation_test.go:15 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessRoute-targetBeforeRun\n  service_validation_test.go:43 TestExecuteRejectsUnsupportedSubprocessReasoningBeforeRun\n  service_runner_dispatch_test.go:41,90,119 TestExecute_Dispatches*\n  service_profiles_test.go:13,61,112 TestServiceProfiles_*\n\nAll same root cause: agent.New() loads the live user config without test isolation.\n\n## Fix paths\n\n1. Tests that don't actually need the user's config should pass an explicit ServiceConfig stub (no upstream-services, or a known shape).\n2. Or, set XDG_CONFIG_HOME to a temp dir in test setup so agent.New() loads an empty config.\n3. Or, add an option to agent.New that skips XDG lookup.\n\nPick whichever is most consistent with how other test-isolation patterns work in this repo.\n\n## Why P2\n\nDoesn't block normal CI (CI clean container has no user config). The WorkerManager in cli/internal/server/workers.go already tracks this (WorkerRecord.CurrentBead at line 51, WorkerRecord.State, WorkerRecord.Runner, WorkerRecord.Route-target, WorkerRecord.Attempts/Successes/Failures) but it is only accessible through the server API. Local --local workers are not tracked by the server at all.\n\nThis bead adds ddx agent workers as a top-level CLI subcommand. It must not depend on worktrunk (wt) or any external tool.\n\n## Required changes\n\n### 1. New command: ddx agent workers (cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go)\nRegister alongside the existing ddx agent log command. Flags:\n  --json         emit raw JSON array\n  --watch        re-render every 2s (like watch -n2) until Ctrl-C\n  --project      filter to a specific project root (default: CWD project)\n\n### 2. Data sources (two paths)\nA. Server-submitted workers: GET http://127.0.0.1:8080/api/agent/workers — deserialize []WorkerRecord from the server. Already implemented in cli/internal/server/workers.go List() / HTTP handler.\nB. Local --local workers: the server does not track these. Detect them by:\n   1. Reading .ddx/workers/*/status.json files in the project root (server writes these even for local workers when they self-register — confirm in workers.go; if not, fall back to scanning active execute-bead worktrees)\n   2. Scanning .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-* directories (from ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant in cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go line 403) and reading the manifest.json inside each to extract bead_id, attempt_id, runner, route-target, started_at\n\n### 3. Local worker detection via manifest scan\nFor each directory matching .ddx/.execute-bead-wt-*:\n  - Read .ddx/executions/{attemptID}/manifest.json (path encoded in worktree name suffix)\n  - Check if worktree is still active (git worktree list --porcelain)\n  - Read execute-loop-heartbeat-at from bead.Extra (requires ddx-7651506a) if available\n  - Derive synthetic WorkerRecord from manifest fields\n\n### 4. Table output (default)\nColumns: STATE | BEAD | TITLE (truncated 40 chars) | RUNNER | ROUTE-TARGET | ELAPSED | ATTEMPTS\n- STATE: running (green), exited (dim), failed (red)\n- ELAPSED: human-readable (e.g., 4m32s)\n- If current bead title unavailable (local worker pre-manifest), show bead ID\n\n### 5. Merge and deduplicate\nWhen both server and manifest sources are available, deduplicate by worker_id or attempt_id. Server record takes precedence over manifest-derived record for the same worker.\n\n## Key files\n- cli/cmd/agent_cmd.go — add newAgentWorkersCommand(), register in newAgentCommand()\n- cli/internal/server/workers.go — WorkerRecord struct (lines 31-54), List API\n- cli/internal/agent/execute_bead.go — ExecuteBeadWtPrefix constant (line 403), manifest shape (lines 726-771)\n- cli/internal/agent/types.go — SessionEntry Correlation map (bead_id field)","acceptance":"ddx agent workers prints a table with STATE, BEAD ID, TITLE, RUNNER, ROUTE-TARGET, ELAPSED, ATTEMPTS columns. A worker executing bead axon-abc123 shows that bead ID and its title (truncated). An idle system (no active workers) prints 'no active workers' rather than an empty table. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:231:{"id":"corpus-f744aa57e91b","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nObserved while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nTighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.\n\n## Scope\n- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn\n- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default\n- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state\n- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary","acceptance":"Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:correctness","area:agent","area:exec","area:git","area:cli"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-55f05e07","title":"Keep execute-bead checkpoint bases free of unrelated DDx runtime churn","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"\u003ccontext-digest\u003e\nObserved while running multiple execute-loop workers concurrently on 2026-04-11. Replacing git stash with checkpoint commits fixed the immediate beads.lock/workers race, but execute-bead still starts from a dirty repo root where .ddx/beads.jsonl and other DDx-owned execution artifacts may be changing because of other live workers. That means one attempt can inherit unrelated DDx runtime/evidence churn into its checkpoint base.\n\u003c/context-digest\u003e\nTighten execute-bead checkpointing so an attempt's input snapshot is not polluted by unrelated DDx-generated state from other live executions.\n\n## Scope\n- Define which DDx-owned paths are part of the execution input snapshot vs excluded runtime churn\n- Prevent unrelated live tracker/evidence/worker mutations from being captured into a new attempt base by default\n- Add concurrency-focused tests covering simultaneous workers and dirty DDx-owned state\n- Keep the tracked execution-evidence contract coherent with the new snapshot boundary","acceptance":"Concurrent execute-bead workers no longer checkpoint unrelated DDx runtime churn from other active attempts into their base revisions; the snapshot boundary for DDx-owned files is documented and enforced; and concurrency tests cover the reproduced multi-worker case.","labels":["ddx","phase:build","kind:correctness","area:agent","area:exec","area:git","area:cli"],"parent":"ddx-32b3008e"}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:232:{"id":"corpus-f897af634136","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"runner-test: build golden-master integration suite for real runneres","description":"Build the integration-level evidence suite for the runner migration. The suite must prove the service can drive each supported live runner and can replay recorded interactions deterministically. This is the answer to whether wrapping Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Pi, and opencode is actually supported and tested.\n\nRequired runneres for this bead: claude, codex, pi, opencode. Gemini may be included if the service dispatch bead wires it and the capability matrix marks it supported.\n\nRequired behaviors to test per runner when supported by its capability matrix:\n- run a prompt through Service.Execute\n- list or explicitly report route-target support/no-discovery semantics\n- pass route-target, reasoning level, permissions, workdir/context, timeout, and metadata\n- capture progress events and final normalized text\n- capture token usage when the runner exposes it\n- capture quota/status data for claude and codex when the capability matrix marks quota supported\n- replay the same interaction sequence identically from cassette without credentials\n\nRecord mode must be opt-in and must fail fast when credentials, subscription, binary, configured route-target, transport dependency, or quota permissions are missing. Replay mode must be runnable in normal CI without live credentials. Ad-hoc fake binaries are acceptable only for shared parser/process infrastructure; they must not be counted as live runner integration evidence.","acceptance":"1. Integration tests are organized around the public capability matrix: every supported required capability is exercised for every required runner, and unsupported optional capabilities are asserted as unavailable.\n2. Record mode can run against live claude, codex, pi, and opencode, fails fast with clear diagnostics when authentication or binaries are missing, and records cassettes with scrubbed environment/credential data.\n3. Replay mode runs deterministically without credentials and verifies event order, progress events, final text, usage where available, quota/status where available, routing metadata, and session log metadata.\n4. Tests cover route-target selection, configured reasoning level, workdir/context behavior, timeout/idle handling, and metadata propagation through Service.Execute.\n5. The suite distinguishes reusable deterministic runner tests from real live-runner evidence and does not claim ad-hoc fake-binary tests as live integration coverage.\n6. Documentation explains how to run record mode and replay mode.\n7. go test -tags=integration ./... passes in replay mode without live credentials.\n8. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:233:{"id":"corpus-fa7f03ad07c9","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"integration: preserve upstream-service identity fallback for upstream-services without ChatStartMetadata","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:182\nCategory: integration\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new chat-span bootstrap path always sources ddx.upstream-service.system from chatStartIdentity. When a upstream-service does not implement ChatStartMetadata, chatStartIdentity returns \"unknown\" instead of preserving the existing session-level upstream-service identity. Before this commit, StartChat seeded Upstream-serviceSystem from sessionUpstream-service, so failed calls from upstream-services that only implement SessionStartMetadata still retained a meaningful identity. This change regresses those error spans to \"unknown\". The repo already has such a upstream-service in upstream-service/virtual/virtual.go, which exposes SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata.\nSuggested fix: Make chatStartIdentity fall back to sessionStartIdentity(upstream-service) for upstream-serviceSystem when ChatStartMetadata is unavailable, while still leaving server address and port unset unless the richer metadata hook is implemented.","acceptance":"When a upstream-service implements SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata and Chat returns an error, the ended chat span records ddx.upstream-service.system with that upstream-service identity instead of unknown, while OpenAI-compatible upstream-services still record resolved server.address and server.port.","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"],"bead":{"id":"hx-36a5e713","title":"integration: preserve upstream-service identity fallback for upstream-services without ChatStartMetadata","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: loop.go:182\nCategory: integration\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new chat-span bootstrap path always sources ddx.upstream-service.system from chatStartIdentity. When a upstream-service does not implement ChatStartMetadata, chatStartIdentity returns \"unknown\" instead of preserving the existing session-level upstream-service identity. Before this commit, StartChat seeded Upstream-serviceSystem from sessionUpstream-service, so failed calls from upstream-services that only implement SessionStartMetadata still retained a meaningful identity. This change regresses those error spans to \"unknown\". The repo already has such a upstream-service in upstream-service/virtual/virtual.go, which exposes SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata.\nSuggested fix: Make chatStartIdentity fall back to sessionStartIdentity(upstream-service) for upstream-serviceSystem when ChatStartMetadata is unavailable, while still leaving server address and port unset unless the richer metadata hook is implemented.","acceptance":"When a upstream-service implements SessionStartMetadata but not ChatStartMetadata and Chat returns an error, the ended chat span records ddx.upstream-service.system with that upstream-service identity instead of unknown, while OpenAI-compatible upstream-services still record resolved server.address and server.port.","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding"]}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:235:{"id":"corpus-fbce190af893","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"correctness: summary since window must ignore late-updated pre-cutoff beads","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: cli/internal/processmetrics/processmetrics.go:285\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new summary window helper uses bead `updated_at` as the inclusion gate, so a bead whose close fact and only correlated sessions are both before the cutoff still appears in `ddx metrics summary --since ...` if it was edited later for unrelated reasons. `beadsVisibleInSummaryWindow` admits that bead, and the downstream summary logic counts it in `summary.Beads.Total/Closed`, `summary.Beads.UnknownCost`, `summary.Cost.Beads`, and `summary.Cost.Features` even though there are no in-window lifecycle or session facts. Repro: with one bead closed on 2026-01-01, one correlated session on 2026-01-01, and only `updated_at=2026-03-05`, `Summary(Query{Since: 2026-02-01, HasSince: true})` returns `beads.total=1 closed=1 unknown_cost=1 cost.beads=1` while sessions, cycle-time, and rework are all zero. SD-016 says `--since` means include only facts observed on or after the cutoff for the relevant fact type; a tracker metadata edit after the window is not a new lifecycle or cost fact.\nSuggested fix: Define summary bead inclusion from the same fact timelines used by the underlying sections, rather than raw `updated_at`. A bead with no in-window close/reopen/session facts should not surface in the windowed summary or its cost/feature counts, and regression coverage should include the late-update/no-facts case.","acceptance":"Given a bead whose first close and only correlated sessions are before the cutoff but whose updated_at is later, metrics summary --since excludes it from bead totals and cost/feature unknown counts; a regression test covers the late-update/no-facts case; cd cli \u0026\u0026 go test ./internal/processmetrics -count=1 passes","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:cli","area:api"],"bead":{"id":"hx-17a99906","title":"correctness: summary since window must ignore late-updated pre-cutoff beads","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Review finding from fresh-eyes review.\nFile: cli/internal/processmetrics/processmetrics.go:285\nCategory: correctness\nSeverity: medium\nDescription: The new summary window helper uses bead `updated_at` as the inclusion gate, so a bead whose close fact and only correlated sessions are both before the cutoff still appears in `ddx metrics summary --since ...` if it was edited later for unrelated reasons. `beadsVisibleInSummaryWindow` admits that bead, and the downstream summary logic counts it in `summary.Beads.Total/Closed`, `summary.Beads.UnknownCost`, `summary.Cost.Beads`, and `summary.Cost.Features` even though there are no in-window lifecycle or session facts. Repro: with one bead closed on 2026-01-01, one correlated session on 2026-01-01, and only `updated_at=2026-03-05`, `Summary(Query{Since: 2026-02-01, HasSince: true})` returns `beads.total=1 closed=1 unknown_cost=1 cost.beads=1` while sessions, cycle-time, and rework are all zero. SD-016 says `--since` means include only facts observed on or after the cutoff for the relevant fact type; a tracker metadata edit after the window is not a new lifecycle or cost fact.\nSuggested fix: Define summary bead inclusion from the same fact timelines used by the underlying sections, rather than raw `updated_at`. A bead with no in-window close/reopen/session facts should not surface in the windowed summary or its cost/feature counts, and regression coverage should include the late-update/no-facts case.","acceptance":"Given a bead whose first close and only correlated sessions are before the cutoff but whose updated_at is later, metrics summary --since excludes it from bead totals and cost/feature unknown counts; a regression test covers the late-update/no-facts case; cd cli \u0026\u0026 go test ./internal/processmetrics -count=1 passes","labels":["helix","phase:build","review-finding","severity:medium","area:cli","area:api"]}}

./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:237:{"id":"corpus-fd7b2f73d368","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"atomic","rationale":"closed with commit evidence and no decomposition signal","title":"execute-bead: audit and enforce per-request HTTP deadlines on agent upstream-service calls","description":"## Root cause RC4 from ddx-0a651925\n\nAfter RC1 is fixed (caller ctx propagates), upstream-service HTTP calls still need per-request deadlines. A stalled TCP socket that delivers headers and then stops sending body bytes — particularly during streaming token reads — can hang a goroutine for days.\n\nGrepping the `DocumentDrivenDX/agent@v0.3.14` module cache shows short timeouts only on discovery probes (`upstream-service/openai/discovery.go:46,174`, `openai.go:123`). The main `Run` call path inside agent upstream-services appears to inherit whatever ctx DDx passes — which today is `context.Background()` (see RC1).\n\n## Scope\n\n1. Audit `openai`, `anthropic`, `virtual` upstream-services in the agent library for per-request deadline usage.\n2. Install a per-request deadline (default ~10-15 min) on each upstream-service call; separate from the wall-clock bound in RC2.\n3. For streaming bodies, enforce an **idle-read limit** on the response body so a mid-stream stall cannot hold a goroutine indefinitely (e.g. `http.Transport.ResponseHeaderTimeout` + periodic `SetReadDeadline` on the underlying conn).\n4. Surface upstream-service-timeout failures as a distinct status in `ExecuteBeadResult.Detail`.\n\n## Non-goals\n\n- Rewriting upstream-services; minimally invasive change only.\n- Changing DDx-side timeout defaults (RC2 covers outer bound).\n\n## Prerequisite\n\nSibling RC1 bead must be resolved first, otherwise adding deadlines inside upstream-services is useless while DDx still feeds them `context.Background()`.","acceptance":"- [ ] Audit report (checked-in .md or bead-comment) lists every `http.Client.Do`-equivalent call in agent upstream-services with its current deadline semantics\n- [ ] Each main upstream-service call path installs a per-request deadline (default 15m or catalog-configurable)\n- [ ] Streaming body reads terminate within a bounded idle period (ReadDeadline or equivalent)\n- [ ] Integration test: fake HTTP server accepts the connection, sends headers, then stops responding. The main `Run` call path inside agent upstream-services appears to inherit whatever ctx DDx passes — which today is `context.Background()` (see RC1).\n\n## Scope\n\n1. Audit `openai`, `anthropic`, `virtual` upstream-services in the agent library for per-request deadline usage.\n2. Install a per-request deadline (default ~10-15 min) on each upstream-service call; separate from the wall-clock bound in RC2.\n3. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:238:{"id":"corpus-011259e83fe4","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","description":"## Observed\n\nUser clicks \"Drain queue\" on the project home page. Mutation returns success with a worker id. Navigating to `/nodes/.../projects/\u003cid\u003e/workers` shows no workers — an empty table.\n\nVerified on disk: the worker record exists and is running.\n\n```\n$ cat .ddx/workers/worker-20260422T205846-f23f/status.json\n{\n  \"id\": \"worker-20260422T205846-f23f\",\n  \"kind\": \"execute-loop\",\n  \"state\": \"running\",\n  \"project_root\": \"/Users/erik/Projects/ddx\",\n  ...\n}\n```\n\nSo the dispatch worked, but the per-project workers list filters it out.\n\n## Root cause\n\n`cli/internal/server/state_graphql.go:156` in `GetWorkersGraphQL`:\n\n```go\nif projectID != \"\" \u0026\u0026 rec.ProjectRoot != projectID {\n    continue\n}\n```\n\n- `projectID` is the GraphQL argument — a project **id** like `proj-96d7ea83`.\n- `rec.ProjectRoot` is the worker record field — a project **path** like `/Users/erik/Projects/ddx`.\n\nThese are different representations and never match. With any non-empty `projectID`, every worker is filtered out.\n\nThe global `workers` resolver (`resolver_agent.go:9`) passes `\"\"` and works correctly. Only the per-project path (`workersByProject` → `GetWorkersGraphQL(projectID)`) is broken. The user's flow (dispatch from project home → navigate to project workers) hits exactly this path.\n\nNote: `GetBeadSnapshots` (state_graphql.go:49-105) handles the same concept correctly by iterating `s.Projects` and filtering on `proj.ID != projectID` — comparing id to id. The worker resolver does not use that pattern.\n\n## Proposed direction\n\nTwo options; prefer (1) for minimal change, (2) for long-term clarity.\n\n### Option 1 — Resolve id → path at the filter site\n\n```go\nexpectedPath := \"\"\nif projectID != \"\" {\n    proj, ok := s.GetProjectByID(projectID)\n    if !ok {\n        return nil // unknown project id → no workers\n    }\n    expectedPath = proj.Path\n}\n...\nif expectedPath != \"\" \u0026\u0026 rec.ProjectRoot != expectedPath {\n    continue\n}\n```\n\n### Option 2 — Store project id on the worker record\n\nAdd `ProjectID string` to `WorkerRecord`. Populate at creation time in `WorkerManager.StartExecuteLoop` via `s.GetProjectByPath(effectiveRoot)`. Filter on `rec.ProjectID != projectID`. Cleaner — the worker record carries its own identity and the query doesn't depend on runtime resolution. Requires a tiny migration (for older records missing the field, fall back to path-resolve as in Option 1).\n\nRecommend Option 2. It removes the representation mismatch for good and is cheap.\n\n## Scope note\n\nThis is a one-line functional bug with outsized impact: it's the reason the Workers page looks broken whenever used as the dashboard intends. Prioritizing P1.\n\n## Out of scope\n\n- Workers pane lifecycle controls (`ddx-69789664`).\n- Cross-project workers view — the global `workers` query already handles that.","acceptance":"**User story:** As an operator, clicking Drain queue on a project home page starts a worker that I can immediately see in that project's Workers view, with its state and current bead updating live.\n\n**Acceptance criteria:**\n\n1. **Root-cause fix.** The `workersByProject(projectID:)` resolver returns all workers whose target project matches the requested id. Both Option 1 (id→path resolution) and Option 2 (store project id on record) satisfy this; implementation chooses one with a one-line justification in the PR description.\n\n2. **Regression test — project-scoped query.**\n   - Seed a ServerState with two registered projects (different paths and ids).\n   - Start one worker for each project.\n   - `workersByProject(projectID: \u003cid-1\u003e)` returns exactly worker 1.\n   - `workersByProject(projectID: \u003cid-2\u003e)` returns exactly worker 2.\n   - `workers` (global) returns both.\n   - Unknown project id returns empty list, not an error.\n\n3. **Regression test — backward compat.**\n   - A worker record on disk without a `ProjectID` field (simulating a pre-migration record) is correctly matched by the path-resolution fallback. Only required if Option 2 is chosen.\n\n4. **End-to-end validation.**\n   - Playwright: navigate to project home, click Drain, confirm, wait 2s, navigate to Workers tab. Assert exactly one new row with `state: running` and matching worker id.\n   - Same test run against a second project asserts that project's Workers tab does not show project-1's worker.\n\n5. **No regression to unrelated surfaces.**\n   - Global `workers` query still returns all workers.\n   - Worker detail page still loads by worker id.\n   - Workers live-progress subscription still patches rows in place.\n\n6. **Cross-reference.**\n   - Note on `ddx-69789664` (Workers lifecycle controls): that bead's acceptance assumed Workers pane worked correctly. Point to this bead as the prerequisite for those tests to be meaningful.","labels":["feat-008","feat-010","bug","graphql"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-05b4cc9d","title":"workersByProject compares project id to project path: per-project workers view always empty","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"## Observed\n\nUser clicks \"Drain queue\" on the project home page. ./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:245:{"id":"corpus-42fe11ad31e3","source_repo":"ddx","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"three or more attempts without a closing commit","title":"work: ready epics are silently skipped by execution loop","description":"In the agent repo on 2026-04-20, `ddx bead ready --json` reported three ready open beads: two epics and one task. The task (`agent-05d81638`) was under retry cooldown until 2026-04-20T06:08:22Z. Running `ddx work --once` started a worker (`worker-20260420T004727-aff9`) that exited with 0 attempts and no log output. Only after running `ddx bead ready --execution --json` was it clear that the execution-ready queue was empty.\n\nThis is confusing operator behavior: `ready` says there is work, but `work` silently does nothing because epics are not execution-eligible. If epics are intentionally container-only, the default ready surface or the work-loop output should make that distinction explicit. If epics are supposed to be executable planning/decomposition targets, the work loop should handle them rather than dropping them.\n\nExpected behavior: when `ddx work` skips ready epics, the operator gets a clear reason and next action, e.g. \"0 execution-ready beads; skipped 2 epics because epics require child task decomposition; 1 task blocked by retry cooldown until \u003ctimestamp\u003e\". Alternatively, `ddx bead ready` should default to execution-ready or visibly mark non-execution-eligible epics so it does not imply `ddx work` can process them.","acceptance":"1. A regression test covers a tracker state with at least one open ready epic and no execution-eligible task beads; `ddx work --once` does not silently exit with an empty log/status.\n2. The operator-facing output or JSON result explains why ready epics were skipped and includes the count/IDs or issue type reason.\n3. `ddx bead ready` and `ddx bead ready --execution` semantics are documented in CLI help or status output so users can predict what `ddx work` will process.\n4. Existing queue/work tests pass.","labels":["area:beads","area:agent","kind:bug","wave:1","workstream:ddx-local"],"bead":{"id":"ddx-672cf2bf","title":"work: ready epics are silently skipped by execution loop","status":"closed","issue_type":"bug","description":"In the agent repo on 2026-04-20, `ddx bead ready --json` reported three ready open beads: two epics and one task. The task (`agent-05d81638`) was under retry cooldown until 2026-04-20T06:08:22Z. Running `ddx work --once` started a worker (`worker-20260420T004727-aff9`) that exited with 0 attempts and no log output. Only after running `ddx bead ready --execution --json` was it clear that the execution-ready queue was empty.\n\nThis is confusing operator behavior: `ready` says there is work, but `work` silently does nothing because epics are not execution-eligible. If epics are intentionally container-only, the default ready surface or the work-loop output should make that distinction explicit. If epics are supposed to be executable planning/decomposition targets, the work loop should handle them rather than dropping them.\n\nExpected behavior: when `ddx work` skips ready epics, the operator gets a clear reason and next action, e.g. \"0 execution-ready beads; skipped 2 epics because epics require child task decomposition; 1 task blocked by retry cooldown until \u003ctimestamp\u003e\". Alternatively, `ddx bead ready` should default to execution-ready or visibly mark non-execution-eligible epics so it does not imply `ddx work` can process them.","acceptance":"1. A regression test covers a tracker state with at least one open ready epic and no execution-eligible task beads; `ddx work --once` does not silently exit with an empty log/status.\n2. Existing queue/work tests pass.","labels":["area:beads","area:agent","kind:bug","wave:1","workstream:ddx-local"]}}
./library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl:246:{"id":"corpus-5078ed4eda9b","source_repo":"agent","expected_class":"decomposable","rationale":"repeated reviewer blocks cite decomposition scope","title":"telemetry: emit override and rejected_override events from Execute","description":"Bead 1 of ADR-006. Emit override and rejected_override events from Execute so the routing-quality signal becomes measurable.\n\nWhen ExecuteRequest has any of Runner, Upstream-service, or Route-target set, the service:\n1. Runs ResolveRoute as today, producing the constrained decision (the dispatched candidate).\n2. Runs a second ResolveRoute with the override axes stripped, producing the unconstrained auto decision.\n3. Emits an 'override' event before the final event with the payload shape from ADR-006 §3 (user_pin, auto_decision, axes_overridden, match_per_axis, auto_score, auto_components, prompt_features, reason_hint, outcome).\n4. Outcome is populated post-execution from the existing final-event status fields.\n\nWhen a pin fails pre-dispatch (orphan route-target, unknown upstream-service, runner/upstream-service incompatibility), emit a 'rejected_override' event with the same shape minus outcome.\n\nprompt_features.estimated_tokens uses ServiceExecuteRequest.EstimatedPromptTokens if provided, else best-effort runner-level tokenizer (nullable).\n\nreason_hint accepts a free-form string from Metadata[\"override.reason\"] (programmatic) or a future --override-reason CLI flag.\n\nIn-scope files:\n- service.go (event payload struct definitions)\n- service_events.go (event type constants + decoded event mapping)\n- service_execute.go (override capture + event emission, second ResolveRoute call)\n- service_execute_test.go (event-shape and emission-order tests)\n\nOut of scope:\n- RouteStatus / UsageReport metric exposure (bead 2)\n- CLI surface (bead 3)\n- Any change to existing routing-decision events\n- Automatic learning loop","acceptance":"1. ExecuteRequest with no override axes set produces no override event (verified in TestExecuteEmitsNoOverrideEventForUnpinnedRequest).\n2. ExecuteRequest with Runner, Upstream-service, or Route-target set produces exactly one override event before the final event. Verified in TestExecuteEmitsOverrideEventBeforeFinal across all three axes individually and in combination.\n3. The override event's auto_decision field is computed from a second ResolveRoute call with the override axes stripped. When pinning matches what unconstrained auto would produce, match_per_axis values are true; the event still fires (coincidental-agreement case). Verified in TestOverrideEventCoincidentalAgreement.\n4. axes_overridden lists exactly the axes the caller pinned (subset of {runner, upstream-service, route-target}). Verified in TestOverrideEventAxesOverriddenIsExplicit.\n5. Outcome is populated post-execution with status, cost_usd, duration_ms matching the final event's values. Verified in TestOverrideEventOutcomePopulatedFromFinal.\n6. Pre-dispatch failure of a pin (orphan route-target, unknown upstream-service) produces a rejected_override event and no override event. Verified in TestRejectedOverrideEventOnOrphanRoute-target.\n7. prompt_features.estimated_tokens reflects ServiceExecuteRequest.EstimatedPromptTokens when set; nullable otherwise. Verified in TestOverrideEventPromptFeaturesPopulation.\n8. reason_hint reflects Metadata[\"override.reason\"] if set; empty string otherwise.\n9. go test ./... -count=1 passes.","labels":["area:service","area:telemetry","kind:feature"],"bead":{"id":"agent-9fc2633c","title":"telemetry: emit override and rejected_override events from Execute","status":"closed","issue_type":"task","description":"Bead 1 of ADR-006. Emit override and rejected_override events from Execute so the routing-quality signal becomes measurable.\n\nWhen ExecuteRequest has any of Runner, Upstream-service, or Route-target set, the service:\n1. Runs ResolveRoute as today, producing the constrained decision (the dispatched candidate).\n2. Runs a second ResolveRoute with the override axes stripped, producing the unconstrained auto decision.\n3. Emits an 'override' event before the final event with the payload shape from ADR-006 §3 (user_pin, auto_decision, axes_overridden, match_per_axis, auto_score, auto_components, prompt_features, reason_hint, outcome).\n4. Outcome is populated post-execution from the existing final-event status fields.\n\nWhen a pin fails pre-dispatch (orphan route-target, unknown upstream-service, runner/upstream-service incompatibility), emit a 'rejected_override' event with the same shape minus outcome.\n\nprompt_features.estimated_tokens uses ServiceExecuteRequest.EstimatedPromptTokens if provided, else best-effort runner-level tokenizer (nullable).\n\nreason_hint accepts a free-form string from Metadata[\"override.reason\"] (programmatic) or a future --override-reason CLI flag.\n\nIn-scope files:\n- service.go (event payload struct definitions)\n- service_events.go (event type constants + decoded event mapping)\n- service_execute.go (override capture + event emission, second ResolveRoute call)\n- service_execute_test.go (event-shape and emission-order tests)\n\nOut of scope:\n- RouteStatus / UsageReport metric exposure (bead 2)\n- CLI surface (bead 3)\n- Any change to existing routing-decision events\n- Automatic learning loop","acceptance":"1. The override event's auto_decision field is computed from a second ResolveRoute call with the override axes stripped. When pinning matches what unconstrained auto would produce, match_per_axis values are true; the event still fires (coincidental-agreement case). Verified in TestOverrideEventOutcomePopulatedFromFinal.\n6. Pre-dispatch failure of a pin (orphan route-target, unknown upstream-service) produces a rejected_override event and no override event. # Triage Gate — Pre-Claim Complexity Evaluator

The triage gate is a pre-Claim phase in `ddx work` that evaluates each candidate
bead before an agent worker claims it. Its purpose is to prevent coarse epics
(beads with multiple independent deliverables) from being dispatched as
monolithic work items, which wastes attempt slots and corrupts claim semantics.

## When the gate triggers

The gate runs for every ready bead, between the routing preflight check and
`Store.Claim`. It evaluates the bead's title, description, acceptance criteria,
and prior attempt history to classify it as:

- **atomic** — a single coherent unit of work; proceeds to Claim normally.
- **decomposable** — multiple independent deliverables; the gate files child
  beads and blocks the parent.
- **ambiguous** — needs human clarification; the gate sets
  `execution-eligible=false` and adds `label=triage:needs-human`.

## Bypassing the gate per-bead

Add the label `triage:skip` to a bead to bypass the complexity gate entirely:

```
ddx bead update <id> --labels triage:skip
```

The bead will proceed directly to Claim on the next dispatch cycle. Use this
for beads you have manually reviewed and confirmed are atomic, or for beads
that the gate misclassifies.

## Decomposed parent state

When the gate decomposes a bead:

1. Child beads are filed with `parent: <id>` linking back to the epic.
2. The parent's status is set to `blocked`.
3. The parent receives a `kind:triage-decomposed` event whose JSON body lists
   the `child_ids` and the splitter's `rationale`.
4. Dependency edges are added: the parent depends on all children, ensuring it
   cannot be dispatched again until children close (should it be re-opened).

Children re-enter the triage gate on their next dispatch cycle. If a child is
itself decomposable, the gate will split it further — up to the depth cap.

## Recursion depth cap

Default depth cap: **3** levels. Configurable in `.ddx/config.yaml` at
`agent.triage.max_decomposition_depth`:

```yaml
agent:
  triage:
    max_decomposition_depth: 3
```

When a bead at the depth cap is evaluated, the gate:

1. Appends a `kind:triage-overflow` event.
2. Sets `status=blocked` with `label=needs-human-decomposition`.
3. Does **not** invoke the classifier or splitter.
4. Does **not** dispatch the bead.

The operator must manually split the bead or add `triage:skip` to bypass.

## AC-coverage metric

The `bead-split` prompt is evaluated against a held-out corpus using a
string-overlap metric: the fraction of unique AC tokens (alphanumeric tokens
≥ 3 chars) from the parent's acceptance criteria that appear in the combined
acceptance criteria of the child specs. A rate ≥ 90% is required.

## Regenerating the historical corpus

The frozen eval slice lives in `library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl`. To
regenerate from live bead history:

```bash
go run ./scripts/triage/harvest-corpus.go \
  --output library/prompts/triage/eval-corpus.jsonl
```

The harvest script reads `.ddx/beads.jsonl` from this repository and includes
`../agent/.ddx/beads.jsonl` when that checkout is present. It produces a
deterministic held-out eval slice. Running it with the same inputs always
produces the same corpus file.

## Ownership boundary

DDx owns orchestration, tracking, work sizing, child bead creation, parent
dependencies, and retry decisions. The agent owns provider/model/harness
routing and execution. The triage gate must not choose or rank concrete
providers, models, or harnesses; if an operator supplied those values, DDx
passes them through as opaque constraints to the agent runtime.

## Disabling the gate

The gate is disabled when `ExecuteBeadWorker.ComplexityGate == nil`. This is
intentionally non-silent: the worker emits a one-time warning per boot:

```
warning: triage complexity gate is disabled; coarse beads may waste dispatch attempts
```

The gate should only be disabled in test fixtures or during development. In
production, always wire a `ComplexityGate` via `NewComplexityGate`.

## Tracker events

| Event kind              | When                                       | Body                          |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| `triage-decomposed`     | Parent split into children                 | `{child_ids, rationale}`      |
| `triage-overflow`       | Bead at depth cap, blocked                 | `{depth, max}`                |
| `triage-ambiguous`      | Gate returned ambiguous classification     | `{confidence, reasoning}`     |
| `triage.gate_disabled`  | Loop boot with nil gate (loop event log)   | `{bead_id}`                   |
| `triage.skipped`        | Gate returned shouldClaim=false            | `{bead_id}`                   |
| `triage.error`          | Gate returned a non-nil error              | `{bead_id, reason}`           |

data.text: I haven’t found any execution-side depth enforcement yet, so I’m checking whether the bead store already exposes ancestry/depth helpers. If it does, I can wire the cap into the worker; if not, this will stay a prompt-only change and the parent triage gate may already be the true enforcement layer.

  <bead id="axon-dccbdf73">
    <title>auth-jwt-pipeline: sign + verify with revocation cache + request extension</title>
    <description>
Implement the full JWT verification middleware in axon-server.

## What you're building

An axum middleware layer that runs on every data-plane request. It validates the JWT, installs a (user_id, tenant_id, grants) tuple into the request extension, and returns a typed AuthError on any failure. Downstream handlers read the extension and trust it completely.

## Scope — exactly what to add

### New file: `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`

A module exposing two things:

1. A `JwtIssuer` helper that signs a `JwtClaims` into a compact JWT string. HS256 is the only algorithm required for this bead. The signing key is a `Vec<u8>` passed in at construction. Provide `issue(&self, claims: &JwtClaims) -> Result<String, AuthError>` and a `verify(&self, token: &str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError>` for round-tripping in tests.

2. An axum middleware `async fn jwt_verify_layer` that runs the 8-step verification order below. On success, installs a `ResolvedIdentity { user_id, tenant_id, grants }` into the request extension. On failure, returns a response built via `AuthError::into_response()` so the mapping to HTTP status + error.code is consistent.

The middleware also owns an `InMemoryRevocationCache` (a small `tokio::sync::RwLock<HashSet<Uuid>>` is fine; nothing fancy) and falls back to SQL (via the storage adapter's new `is_jti_revoked` method) on cache miss.

### Extend: `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs`

- Add `ResolvedIdentity { pub user_id: UserId, pub tenant_id: TenantId, pub grants: Grants }`.
- Add `impl AuthError` with `pub fn status_code(&self) -> u16` returning 401 or 403 per ADR-018 §4 failure table, and `pub fn error_code(&self) -> &'static str` returning the stable code string. (An axum `IntoResponse` impl can live in axon-server.)
- Add `impl Grants` with `pub fn find_database(&self, name: &str) -> Option<&GrantedDatabase>` and `impl GrantedDatabase` with `pub fn has_op(&self, op: Op) -> bool`.

### Extend: `crates/axon-storage/src/adapter.rs` (trait)

Add one method to the `StorageAdapter` trait:

```rust
async fn is_jti_revoked(&self, jti: Uuid) -> Result<bool, AxonError>;
```

Implement it in `crates/axon-storage/src/memory.rs` (store a `HashSet<Uuid>`; default-empty), `crates/axon-storage/src/sqlite.rs`, and `crates/axon-storage/src/postgres.rs` (`SELECT 1 FROM credential_revocations WHERE jti = $1`). The A1 schema (`auth_schema.rs`) already has the `credential_revocations` table — just query it.

### Wire into: `crates/axon-server/src/lib.rs`

Re-export `auth_pipeline::{jwt_verify_layer, JwtIssuer, InMemoryRevocationCache}`. Don't wire it into the main router yet — that's D-phase work. For this bead, the middleware just needs to exist and be testable in isolation.

## Verification order (normative — ADR-018 §4)

1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` from the request. Missing header → `AuthError::Unauthenticated` (401). The `--no-auth` bypass is NOT part of this bead — that's E2.
2. Parse and verify the JWT signature with HS256 against the issuer's key. Signature invalid → `CredentialInvalid` (401). Malformed structure → `CredentialMalformed` (401). `aud` as a JSON array → `CredentialMalformed` (this falls out of the A2 `parse_claims` wrapper — use it).
3. Check `nbf` and `exp` against `SystemTime::now()` with a 30s skew budget. `exp` more than 30s in the past → `CredentialExpired` (401). `nbf` more than 30s in the future → `CredentialNotYetValid` (401).
4. Check `claims.jti` against the revocation cache, then SQL on miss. Hit → `CredentialRevoked` (401). Populate the cache on SQL hit to speed up subsequent checks.
5. Compare `claims.aud` to the `{tenant}` segment extracted from the URL path. Mismatch → `CredentialWrongTenant` (**403**, not 401 — see the table). The URL extraction path is: look at `request.uri().path()` and parse the leading `/tenants/{tenant}/databases/{database}/...` segments. For this bead, implement a small helper `fn extract_tenant_database(path: &str) -> Option<(&str, &str)>` that returns `None` for non-data-plane paths so they skip the middleware.
6. Look up the `sub` UUID in the `users` table via a new `StorageAdapter::get_user(UserId) -> Result<Option<User>, AxonError>` method. User not found or `user.suspended_at_ms.is_some()` → `UserSuspended` (401).
7. Check tenant membership: a new `StorageAdapter::get_tenant_member(TenantId, UserId) -> Result<Option<TenantMember>, AxonError>`. Missing → `NotATenantMember` (403).
8. Walk `claims.grants.databases[]` looking for an entry matching the URL `{database}`. None → `DatabaseNotGranted` (403). Found → compute the required op from the HTTP method (GET = Read, POST/PUT/PATCH/DELETE = Write) and intersect with the grant's ops. Empty intersection → `OpNotGranted` (403). Admin-requiring routes are out of scope for this bead — the middleware only handles Read/Write classification.
9. Build `ResolvedIdentity` and install it via `request.extensions_mut().insert(resolved)`.

## Error mapping — must exactly match ADR-018 §4

The mapping table is copied here verbatim. Do not invent variants; the 14 AuthError variants already exist in axon-core.

| Failure | Status | `error.code` |
|---|---|---|
| No Authorization header | 401 | `unauthenticated` |
| Header not `Bearer <token>` or base64 broken | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| JWT structurally invalid / missing claims | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| `aud` as JSON array | 401 | `credential_malformed` |
| Signature invalid | 401 | `credential_invalid` |
| `exp` in past (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_expired` |
| `nbf` in future (beyond skew) | 401 | `credential_not_yet_valid` |
| `jti` revoked | 401 | `credential_revoked` |
| `iss` unknown | 401 | `credential_foreign_issuer` — for this bead, reject if `claims.iss != issuer.issuer_id` |
| `aud` ≠ URL tenant | **403** | `credential_wrong_tenant` |
| `sub` suspended/deleted | 401 | `user_suspended` |
| `sub` not a tenant member | **403** | `not_a_tenant_member` |
| URL database not in grants | **403** | `database_not_granted` |
| Op not in grant | **403** | `op_not_granted` |

Note the 401 vs 403 split: credential-level problems are 401, scope-level problems are 403.

## Testing — all must pass for this bead to be complete

### Unit tests in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_test.rs`

For every row of the error mapping table, build the minimal defective request and assert the middleware produces exactly that `(status, error_code)` pair. Table-driven with `#[test]` or a single `#[test]` that iterates an array of cases.

Additionally:

- `jwt_roundtrip_succeeds`: issue a valid JWT with `JwtIssuer::issue`, verify it with `JwtIssuer::verify`, assert claims match.
- `valid_jwt_populates_request_extension`: run the full middleware on a valid JWT, assert the response is 200 and the handler sees `ResolvedIdentity` in extensions.
- `revocation_takes_effect_within_one_second`: issue a valid JWT, verify it succeeds, insert the jti into the revocation cache, verify the next request returns 401 `credential_revoked`. Do NOT use sleep — invalidate the cache directly.
- `clock_skew_30s_accepted_31s_rejected`: construct a JWT with `exp` at `now - 29s` (should succeed) and one with `exp` at `now - 31s` (should fail with `credential_expired`). Do the same for `nbf`.
- `ops_matrix`: for each HTTP method in {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE} and each op set in the grant, assert the expected 200/403 outcome.

### Integration test in `crates/axon-server/tests/auth_pipeline_integration_test.rs`

Build a minimal axum router with one dummy route `GET /tenants/acme/databases/orders/ping`. Install the middleware. Hit it with:
- No auth header → 401 `unauthenticated`
- Valid JWT with `grants: [{name: "orders", ops: [read]}]` → 200
- Valid JWT with the wrong tenant in aud → 403 `credential_wrong_tenant`
- Valid JWT with grants for a different database → 403 `database_not_granted`

### axon-sim workload `inv_018_grant_enforcement`

**If this is too large for one pass, skip the axon-sim workload and note it in the commit message for a follow-up bead.** The core verification must ship; the simulation workload can follow. Do not block the whole bead on the workload.

## Crate dependencies you will need

Add to `crates/axon-server/Cargo.toml` under `[dependencies]`:
```toml
jsonwebtoken = "9"
```

Everything else (axum, tower, tokio, uuid, serde_json, http) is already in the workspace.

## Context you must know about

### A1 landed: schema
- `crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs` has the SQL for `users`, `user_identities`, `tenant_users`, `credential_revocations`, etc.
- `user_identities.external_id` (NOT `subject`) — already corrected.
- Migration runs via `apply_auth_migrations_sqlite` / `_postgres`.

### A2 landed: types
- `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` has all the types: `TenantId`, `UserId`, `User`, `UserIdentity`, `Tenant`, `TenantMember`, `TenantRole`, `Op`, `GrantedDatabase`, `Grants`, `JwtClaims`, `AuthError` (14 variants).
- `parse_claims(&str) -> Result<JwtClaims, AuthError>` wraps serde_json and maps errors to `CredentialMalformed`. USE IT.
- `AUTH_ERROR_VARIANT_COUNT == 14` is enforced via compile-time exhaustive match.
- Delegation helper on `TenantRole`: `admin.can_delegate(op)` / etc.

### Files you should NOT touch
- Anything outside `crates/axon-server/src/auth_pipeline.rs`, `crates/axon-core/src/auth.rs` (additive), `crates/axon-storage/src/{adapter,memory,sqlite,postgres}.rs` (additive), and the two new test files.
- The existing `crates/axon-server/src/auth.rs` — that's the Tailscale whois path. Leave it alone. Your pipeline is a new module.

You are the implementation step. A post-merge review with opus will verify the result against the AC. If your commit misses an AC item, the bead reopens and escalates to a higher tier. **Don't commit partial work hoping the review slides.** If you cannot complete the bead in this pass, write a `no_changes_rationale.txt` explaining exactly what's done and what's blocking, and do NOT commit — the bead will be re-queued.

Specifically: if you cannot make `cargo test -p axon-server` and `cargo clippy -p axon-server -- -D warnings` green, do not commit. Fix the issues first.

Then read crates/axon-storage/src/auth_schema.rs to confirm the credential_revocations table shape. Then start coding.

- Do not modify CLAUDE.md or add new documentation.

## Done definition

`cargo test -p axon-server -p axon-core -p axon-storage` green + `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` clean + every AC item ticked + one commit. That's done.

{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01DGpwMtL9EAnaRPBK3GqEBs","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t//! Axon server — HTTP gateway, gRPC service, MCP stdio, and authentication.\n2\t//!\n3\t//! # Authentication\n4\t//!\n5\t//! Identity is resolved once per request by [`auth::AuthContext`] and\n6\t//! injected as a typed extension.  Three modes are supported:\n7\t//!\n8\t//! | Mode | Flag | Actor | Role |\n9\t//! |------|------|-------|------|\n10\t//! | `NoAuth` | `--no-auth` | `\"anonymous\"` | Admin |\n11\t//! | `Tailscale` | *(default)* | node name | from ACL tags |\n12\t//! | `Guest` | `--guest-role <role>` | `\"guest\"` | configured role |\n13\t//!\n14\t//! In `Tailscale` mode the server contacts the local Tailscale daemon over its\n15\t//! Unix socket (`/run/tailscale/tailscaled.sock` by default) and calls\n16\t//! `/localapi/v0/whois?addr=<peer>` to resolve the connecting node's identity.\n17\t//! Resolved identities are cached by peer IP for the duration of\n18\t//! `--auth-cache-ttl-secs` (default 60 s).\n19\t//!\n20\t//! ACL tag → role mapping:\n21\t//! - `tag:axon-admin` → [`Role::Admin`]\n22\t//! - `tag:axon-write` / `tag:axon-agent` → [`Role::Write`]\n23\t//! - `tag:axon-read` → [`Role::Read`]\n24\t//! - no matching tag → `--tailscale-default-role` (default `read`)\n25\t//!\n26\t//! Connections that are not on the tailnet are rejected with HTTP 401 / gRPC\n27\t//! `UNAUTHENTICATED`.  If the Tailscale daemon is unreachable the server\n28\t//! returns HTTP 503 / gRPC `UNAVAILABLE`.\n29\t//!\n30\t//! # Request flow\n31\t//!\n32\t//! ```text\n33\t//! TCP accept\n34\t//!   └─ authenticate_http_request  (gateway.rs middleware)\n35\t//!        ├─ AuthContext::resolve_peer  →  Identity\n36\t//!        └─ insert Identity into request extensions\n37\t//!             └─ route handler\n38\t//!                  ├─ extract Extension<Identity>\n39\t//!                  ├─ identity.require_read() / require_write() / require_admin()\n40\t//!                  └─ handler logic\n41\t//! ```\n42\t//!\n43\t//! gRPC follows the same pattern via [`service::AxonServiceImpl::authorize`].\n44\tpub mod actor_scope;\n45\tpub mod auth;\n46\tmod collection_listing;\n47\tpub(crate) mod embedded_ui;\n48\tpub mod control_plane;\n49\tpub mod control_plane_routes;\n50\tpub mod cors_config;\n51\tpub mod user_roles;\n52\tpub mod gateway;\n53\tpub mod idempotency;\n54\tpub(crate) mod mcp_http;\n55\tpub mod mcp_stdio;\n56\tpub mod rate_limit;\n57\tpub mod schema_registry;\n58\tpub mod serve;\n59\tpub mod service;\n60\tpub mod tenant_router;\n61\t\n62\tpub use auth::{AuthContext, AuthMode, Identity, Role};\n63\tpub use mcp_stdio::run_mcp_stdio;\n64\tpub use service::AxonServiceImpl;\n65\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":"toolu_01TSMSYvQzdh78YH3Sxyiy8q","session_id":"80f0eb9d-4064-4d06-8560-e247ce1170f2","uuid":"b2e10ba8-f883-42a2-b408-1004661a2ce3","timestamp":"2026-04-15T01:30:53.127Z"}

We apply a conservative subset of that\n1184\t/// rule here to keep things simple and safe.\n1185\tfn validate_pg_db_name(name: &str) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n1186\t    if name.is_empty() {\n1187\t        return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(\n1188\t            \"database name must not be empty\".to_owned(),\n1189\t        ));\n1190\t    }\n1191\t    let first = name.chars().next().expect(\"non-empty checked above\");\n1192\t    if !first.is_ascii_alphabetic() && first != '_' {\n1193\t        return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(format!(\n1194\t            \"database name '{name}' must start with a letter or underscore\"\n1195\t        )));\n1196\t    }\n1197\t    if !name.chars().all(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '_') {\n1198\t        return Err(AxonError::InvalidArgument(format!(\n1199\t            \"database name '{name}' contains invalid characters (only ASCII alphanumeric and _ allowed)\"\n1200\t        )));\n1201\t    }\n1202\t    Ok(())\n1203\t}\n1204\t\n1205\t/// Derive a per-tenant DSN from a superadmin DSN by replacing/adding the\n1206\t/// `dbname` key.\n1207\t///\n1208\t/// The superadmin DSN uses the `postgres` maintenance database.  We produce a\n1209\t/// new DSN that targets `axon_{name}`.\n1210\t///\n1211\t/// Both libpq keyword–value format (`host=... dbname=...`) and URL format\n1212\t/// (`postgres://...`) are supported.\n1213\tpub fn tenant_dsn(superadmin_dsn: &str, tenant_db_name: &str) -> String {\n1214\t    let target = format!(\"axon_{tenant_db_name}\");\n1215\t    if superadmin_dsn.starts_with(\"postgres://\") || superadmin_dsn.starts_with(\"postgresql://\") {\n1216\t        // URL format: replace path component.\n1217\t        // e.g. postgres://user:pass@host/somedb?sslmode=disable\n1218\t        if let Some(pos) = superadmin_dsn.find(\"://\") {\n1219\t            let after_scheme = &superadmin_dsn[pos + 3..];\n1220\t            // Find the slash that separates authority from path.\n1221\t            if let Some(slash_pos) = after_scheme.find('/') {\n1222\t                let scheme_authority = &superadmin_dsn[..pos + 3 + slash_pos];\n1223\t                let rest = &after_scheme[slash_pos + 1..];\n1224\t                // Strip existing dbname (everything before '?').\n1225\t                let query = if let Some(q) = rest.find('?') {\n1226\t                    &rest[q..]\n1227\t                } else {\n1228\t                    \"\"\n1229\t                };\n1230\t                return format!(\"{scheme_authority}/{target}{query}\");\n1231\t            }\n1232\t            // No path: append the target database.\n1233\t            return format!(\"{superadmin_dsn}/{target}\");\n1234\t        }\n1235\t        format!(\"{superadmin_dsn}/{target}\")\n1236\t    } else {\n1237\t        // Keyword–value format.  Replace existing dbname= or append it.\n1238\t        let mut parts: Vec<String> = superadmin_dsn\n1239\t            .split_whitespace()\n1240\t            .filter(|part| !part.starts_with(\"dbname=\"))\n1241\t            .map(str::to_owned)\n1242\t            .collect();\n1243\t        parts.push(format!(\"dbname={target}\"));\n1244\t        parts.join(\" \")\n1245\t    }\n1246\t}\n1247\t\n1248\t/// Create a physical PostgreSQL database named `axon_{name}` using a\n1249\t/// superadmin connection.\n1250\t///\n1251\t/// The `superadmin_dsn` must connect to the `postgres` maintenance database\n1252\t/// (or any database the superuser can reach) with sufficient privileges to\n1253\t/// execute `CREATE DATABASE`.\n1254\t///\n1255\t/// The database name is validated to prevent SQL injection.\n1256\t///\n1257\t/// # Errors\n1258\t///\n1259\t/// Returns `AxonError::AlreadyExists` if the database already exists.\n1260\t/// Returns `AxonError::InvalidArgument` if `name` contains invalid characters.\n1261\t/// Returns `AxonError::Storage` for other PostgreSQL errors.\n1262\tpub fn provision_postgres_database(superadmin_dsn: &str, name: &str) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n1263\t    validate_pg_db_name(name)?;\n1264\t    let full_name = format!(\"axon_{name}\");\n1265\t    let rt = tokio::runtime::Builder::new_current_thread()\n1266\t        .enable_all()\n1267\t        .build()\n1268\t        .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\n1269\t    rt.block_on(async {\n1270\t        let (client, connection) = tokio_postgres::connect(superadmin_dsn, NoTls)\n1271\t            .await\n1272\t            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\n1273\t        tokio::spawn(async move {\n1274\t            let _ = connection.await;\n1275\t        });\n1276\t        let row = client\n1277\t            .query_one(\n1278\t                \"SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM pg_database WHERE datname = $1)\",\n1279\t                &[&full_name.as_str()],\n1280\t            )\n1281\t            .await\n1282\t            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\n1283\t        let exists: bool = row.get(0);\n1284\t        if exists {\n1285\t            return Err(AxonError::AlreadyExists(format!(\n1286\t                \"PostgreSQL database '{full_name}'\"\n1287\t            )));\n1288\t        }\n1289\t        // CREATE DATABASE cannot run inside a transaction; use simple_query to\n1290\t        // avoid the implicit transaction that execute() would open.\n1291\t        client\n1292\t            .simple_query(&format!(\"CREATE DATABASE \\\"{full_name}\\\"\"))\n1293\t            .await\n1294\t            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\n1295\t        Ok(())\n1296\t    })\n1297\t}\n1298\t\n1299\t/// Drop the physical PostgreSQL database named `axon_{name}`.\n1300\t///\n1301\t/// The {\n1317\t        let (client, connection) = tokio_postgres::connect(superadmin_dsn, NoTls)\n1318\t            .await\n1319\t            .map_err(|e| AxonError::Storage(e.to_string()))?;\n1320\t        tokio::spawn(async move {\n1321\t            let _ = connection.await;\n1322\t        });\n1323\t        let row = client\n1324\t            .query_one(\n1325\t                \"SELECT EXISTS(SELECT 1 Role-based and attribute-based access control for Axon (FEAT-012).\n1\t//!\n2\t//! # Layers\n3\t//!\n4\t//! **Layer 1 — RBAC** ([`Role`], [`CallerIdentity`], [`Operation`])\n5\t//!\n6\t//! Four built-in roles (`Admin > Write > Read > None`) control access to\n7\t//! Axon operations.  Roles are derived from the identity provider (Tailscale\n8\t//! ACL tags, OIDC claims, etc.) by the server layer and passed into handlers\n9\t//! as a [`CallerIdentity`].  Handlers call [`CallerIdentity::check`] to\n10\t//! enforce the minimum required role.\n11\t//!\n12\t//! **Layer 2 — Field-level masking** ([`MaskPolicy`])\n13\t//!\n14\t//! A `MaskPolicy` hides individual entity fields from callers whose role\n15\t//! falls below a configured minimum.  Applied by [`CallerIdentity::apply_masks`]\n16\t//! before returning entity data to the caller.  Admins always see all fields.\n17\t//!\n18\t//! **Layer 3 — Collection write control** ([`WritePolicy`])\n19\t//!\n20\t//! A `WritePolicy` sets a per-collection minimum write role and marks fields\n21\t//! as immutable after creation.  Checked by the handler before any mutation.\n22\t//!\n23\t//! **Layer 4 — Database-scoped grants** ([`DatabaseGrant`], [`GrantRegistry`])\n24\t//!\n25\t//! Grants scope a role to a specific database (or `\"*\"` for all databases).\n26\t//! Without a matching grant a caller has `Role::None` on that database.\n27\t//! Admins with a global grant bypass per-database checks.  The grant registry\n28\t//! is stored in `__axon_policies__` and enforced at the routing layer.\n29\t\n30\tuse serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};\n31\t\n32\tuse crate::error::AxonError;\n33\t\n34\t/// Built-in roles for Axon RBAC (FEAT-012).\n35\t///\n36\t/// Roles are ordered by privilege: `Admin > Write > Read > None`.\n37\t/// When a user has multiple roles (e.g., from multiple ACL tags),\n38\t/// the highest-privilege role wins.\n39\t#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord, Hash, Serialize, Deserialize)]\n40\tpub enum Role {\n41\t    /// No access. Explicitly denied.\n42\t    None = 0,\n43\t    /// Read entities, query, traverse, browse audit log.\n44\t    Read = 1,\n45\t    /// Create, update, delete entities and links in any collection.\n46\t    Write = 2,\n47\t    /// All operations including drop and schema changes.\n48\t    Admin = 3,\n49\t}\n50\t\n51\timpl Role {\n52\t    /// Parse a role from a Tailscale ACL tag or string name.\n53\t    ///\n54\t    /// Accepted inputs (both full `tag:` prefix and bare name):\n55\t    ///\n56\t    /// | Input | Role |\n57\t    /// |-------|------|\n58\t    /// | `\"tag:axon-admin\"` / `\"tag:admin\"` / `\"admin\"` | `Admin` |\n59\t    /// | `\"tag:axon-write\"` / `\"tag:axon-agent\"` / `\"tag:write\"` / `\"write\"` | `Write` |\n60\t    /// | `\"tag:axon-read\"` / `\"tag:read\"` / `\"read\"` | `Read` |\n61\t    /// | anything else | `None` |\n62\t    ///\n63\t    /// `tag:axon-agent` is an alias for `write` — the conventional tag for\n64\t    /// automated agent workloads that need read/write but not admin access.\n65\t    pub fn from_tag(tag: &str) -> Self {\n66\t        match tag {\n67\t            \"tag:axon-admin\" | \"tag:admin\" | \"admin\" => Role::Admin,\n68\t            \"tag:axon-write\" | \"tag:axon-agent\" | \"tag:write\" | \"write\" => Role::Write,\n69\t            \"tag:axon-read\" | \"tag:read\" | \"read\" => Role::Read,\n70\t            _ =>   Self(val.to_bits())\n48\t    }\n49\t\n50\t    pub fn value(&self) -> f64 {\n51\t        f64::from_bits(self.0)\n52\t    }\n53\t}\n54\t\n55\timpl PartialEq for OrderedFloat {\n56\t    fn eq(&self, current_after_race.map(Box::new),\n            });\n        }\n\n        Ok(Entity {\n            collection: key.collection,\n            version: new_version,\n            ..entity\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn create_if_absent(\n        &mut self,\n        entity: Entity,\n        expected_absent_version: u64,\n    ) -> Result<Entity, AxonError> {\n        let key = self.resolve_catalog_key(&entity.collection)?;\n        let data_json = serde_json::to_value(&entity.data)?;\n        let changed = self.block(self.client.execute(\n            \"INSERT INTO entities (collection, database_name, schema_name, id, version, data)\n                 VALUES ($1, $2, $3, $4, $5, $6)\n                 ON CONFLICT (database_name, schema_name, collection, id) DO NOTHING\",\n            &[\n                &key.collection.as_str(),\n                &key.namespace.database,\n                &key.namespace.schema,\n                &entity.id.as_str(),\n                &(entity.version as i64),\n                &data_json,\n            ],\n        ))?;\n\n        if changed == 0 {\n            let current = self.get(&entity.collection, &entity.id)?;\n            let actual = current\n                .as_ref()\n                .map(|existing| existing.version)\n                .unwrap_or(0);\n            return Err(AxonError::ConflictingVersion {\n                expected: expected_absent_version,\n                actual,\n                current_entity: current.map(Box::new),\n            });\n        }\n\n        Ok(Entity {\n            collection: key.collection,\n            ..entity\n        })\n    }\n\n    fn begin_tx(&mut self) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if self.in_tx {\n            return Err(AxonError::Storage(\"transaction already active\".into()));\n        }\n        self.block(self.client.batch_execute(\"BEGIN\"))?;\n        self.in_tx = true;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn commit_tx(&mut self) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if !self.in_tx {\n            return Err(AxonError::Storage(\"no active transaction\".into()));\n        }\n        self.block(self.client.batch_execute(\"COMMIT\"))?;\n        self.in_tx = false;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn abort_tx(&mut self) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if !self.in_tx {\n            return Ok(());\n        }\n        self.block(self.client.batch_execute(\"ROLLBACK\"))?;\n        self.in_tx = false;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn create_database(&mut self, name: &str) -> Result<(), AxonError> {\n        if self.client\n                .query(\"SELECT name FROM databases ORDER BY name ASC\", &[]),\n        )?;\n        Ok(rows.iter().map(|row| row.get(\"name\")).collect())\n    }\n\n    fn ))?;\n        Ok(())\n    }\n\n    fn list_namespace_collections(\n        &self,\n        namespace: &Namespace,\n    ) -> Result<Vec<CollectionId>, AxonError> {\n        if
